item.title: The Benefits of the Binary Search Algorithm
item.author: meetnkothari13@gmail.com (Meet Kothari)
item.description:
Introduction

As computer scientists, oftentimes, we deal with the concept of scope. Rarely, if ever, is our scope limited to just one use-case. In that same vein of thought, rarely, if ever, do we look at situations and concepts in a vacuum, unconnected to possible avenues in the future. One of the tenets of the trade is scalability; what good is an algorithm that only works once? Now, assuming your algorithm passes the “vacuum” test and can be deployed in several different situations, that’s just step one. There’s a dozen or so other steps that follow—should you really want to implement a novel algorithm—but even a journey that large must be condensed into smaller facets. Though it does depend on who you ask, it’s a widely held belief that one of these facets is efficiency; the more efficient an algorithm is, the better. An efficient algorithm results in less iterations; it directly leads to less time being spent; less space being used. Situations can differ, sure. Perhaps you’re trying to research different algorithms in an isolated setting. Maybe you’re developing a new sorting method for a professional setting. Regardless of the situation at hand, whenever you aim to use an algorithm, scalability and efficiency should be at the forefront of your mind.

Enter, the Binary Search algorithm. The Binary Search algorithm seems to, at a fundamental level, fly in the face of this mentality—at least, partially. In terms of scalability, admittedly, the Binary Search algorithm maintains a high standard. The recurrence relation for the Binary Search is $$ [ T(n) = 1 \cdot T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1) ] $$

where the $1T$ corresponds to the number of subproblems, the $n/2$ correlates to the size of the subproblems, and the $O(1)$ corresponds to the work dividing and combining. Its time complexity is $O(log n)$—this means that, simultaneously, as the size of the input array $n$ increases, the amount of time required to search for an element in the array increases at a slower rate. Furthermore, to add to the supremacy of the algorithm, the space complexity of the Binary Search algorithm is $O(1)$, or constant time. This means that the memory required by the algorithm remains the same regardless of the size of the input array. This is because the algorithm only requires a few variables while being deployed. In most iterations of the algorithm, the only variables that are expressly needed are the variables used to store information about the search; this includes, but is not limited to, the lower and upper bounds of the search space, and the index of the middle element. Given the efficient time complexity and the constant space complexity, the Binary Search is a very attractive algorithm for searching large arrays or other sets of data. Furthermore, the Binary Search algorithm can be easily parallelized, as the search space can be divided into multiple parts, each of which can be searched independently. This allows the algorithm to be run on multiple processors or cores, which can significantly reduce the search time. Though, since it does fall under the umbrella of what’s referred to as “divide and conquer” algorithms, the Binary Search algorithm does require that the input array be sorted in ascending or descending order before it can be applied. This may seem counterintuitive at first, as one might expect a search algorithm to work regardless of the order of the input. However, sorting the input array is a necessary step to divide the search space into two halves and locate the target element efficiently. As one would imagine, this becomes quite the task when the sets of data in use grow larger. Still, the trade-off becomes clear: spend a little time up front, save a lot of time in the back half. But is it really that simple?

Applications

At first, the generalization of the Binary Search may seem like both a benefit and a detriment at the same time. Being capable of the scalability provided by the recurrence relation sets the table for a world where the Binary Search is utilized in every facet of large-data sorting. Though, as history will show, there is a specific niche that has been carved out for this algorithm.

The Binary Search shines when it’s used in tandem with other algorithms. Take “A BinarySearch-Based Locality-Sensitive Hashing Method for Cross-Site User Identification [2]” into account, for example. The article presents a novel method for cross-site user identification using locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) and binary search techniques. The proposed method aims to identify users across multiple sites using only their common behavior patterns. This would, in essence, remove the necessity for privileged knowledge—personally identifiable information, that is, which contains everything from a person’s full name, username, to their email address, phone number, and even photos or videos that show their face. The authors use LSH to generate hash codes for the users’ behavior patterns and store them in a binary search tree. They also use binary search techniques to identify matching users across different sites efficiently. These techniques excel in this situation, given their propensity for sorting large sums of data. As one would expect, identifying users across sites results in an incredibly large vat of data to sort through. Furthermore, needing to adjust the number of sites being used in the study, or even the range of users sampled becomes trivial when the Binary Search is considered. Indeed, the set-up time needed for the algorithm allows for the experiment to be adjusted as needed. As stated by in the article, the flexibility of the Binary Search “[alleviates] the complexity of processing user trajectory […] our BLSH encoding is quite flexible due to the binary code’s adjustable length, which totally depends on the precision requirement. [2]” This study illustrates the true boon of using the Binary Search, the elasticity of the algorithm. It’s basically a Swiss army knife in the sense that it can easily adjust to changes in the size of the dataset, range of values, or number of search queries without requiring significant changes to the algorithm’s implementation. Initially, what seems counterintuitive in the form of a precursory setup, ends up being a major benefit in situations where the fields being evaluated are subject to change.

Continuing the trend of elasticity within the algorithm, the article “Multidimensional Binary Search Trees Used for Associative Searching [3]” describes the use of binary search trees for associative searching in multidimensional data structures. The article explains how to create a binary search tree and use it to search for data that is associated with specific keys, and how this method can be used in various applications such as computer graphics and databases. The basic premise of this approach is creating nodes to house the information being retrieved. The article also discusses how if the “if the nodes were sorted by state at the time of file creation […] using random insertion in this case would probably produce a terribly unbalanced tree. [3]” This displays another strength of the Binary Search: the deliberate nature of its output. In the example from before, nodes were being created and then, at the time of creation, being sorted through insertion. The issue that arises when dealing with insertion, though, is that insertion sort works by repeatedly taking an element from the input array and inserting it into the correct position in the sorted portion of the array. When used to build a binary search tree, insertion sort will first insert the smallest value in the array as the root of the tree. Then, it will insert the next smallest value as the left child of the root, and so on. The problem with this approach is that if the input array is already sorted or nearly sorted, the tree that results from using insertion sort will be unbalanced. Specifically, the tree will be a degenerate case where each node has only one child, leading to a linked list structure rather than a balanced binary tree. In direct contrast, Binary Search uses the divide-and-conquer method, which allows it to slash the search range in half with each comparison, leading to a balanced partitioning of the input data. In other words, when you use the Binary Search algorithm instead of, say, insertion sort, you can count on your created output being balanced by design. That kind of power can save a lot of time.

Lastly, this paper mentioned the Binary Search’s premise of future scalability earlier. “Combining Dynamic K-Means and Binary Search Centroid to Optimize Clustering Results on Home Industry Datasets [4]” is an excellent example of how even the base version of the Binary Search Algorithm can be built upon and used to perform dense computational tasks. To summarize the article, it’s about a study that combines two algorithms to optimize clustering results for datasets. The study aims to improve the efficiency of clustering in industries by using these algorithms. One of the ways they do this is by implementing a Binary Search, in addition to their original algorithm. By the author’s own admission, “[…] the K-means algorithm requires a specific number of clusters. Furthermore, the K-means algorithm is sensitive when it comes to determining the initial value of the centroid and the number of initial cluster partitions […] as a result, if the random value used for the centroid initialization is poor, the grouping results will be suboptimal. [4]” The Binary Search component of this algorithm assists it in finding that “centroid” value. As highlighted in the previous example, the Binary Search algorithm excels at creating inherently balanced outputs. So, especially when used in tandem with the K-means algorithm, it can combine the computational power of the base algorithm with the secure scalability of the Binary Search—since the grouping results will be inherently balanced, that allows Santoso to, later in the article, collect and build upon larger and larger swathes of data, all while maintaining “[the lowest] DBI value of 0.1845…[4]”

Conclusions

In conclusion, the Binary Search algorithm is a highly efficient algorithm that offers scalability and constant space complexity. Although it requires that the input array is sorted, the time saved in the search process is well worth the time spent sorting the array. Beyond that, that setup time also affords the user of the algorithm a means of controlling how much data is stored and added—this benefits the flexibility of the algorithm, as it can be scaled up or down, and the input variables can be changed on a whim. The Binary Search algorithm has shown its utility in various applications, especially when used in conjunction with other algorithms. One such example is the LSH method for cross-site user identification, which employs the Binary Search to sort through large amounts of data efficiently. Another such example is the K-means and Binary Search combo, which uses both algorithms to create a behemoth in computational power with exponential scalability without compromising any performance or efficiency. In essence, the flexibility and efficiency of the Binary Search algorithm make it a valuable tool in the computer science field, and its continued use in various applications is expected.

References

[1] T. H. Cormen, C. E. Leiserson, R. L. Rivest, and C. Stein, “Introduction to Algorithms.” The MIT Press, 2009, ISBN: 0262033844.
[2] W. He, Y. Li, Y. Zhang, and X. Li, “A Binary-Search-Based Locality-Sensitive Hashing Method for Cross-Site User Identification,” in IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security, vol. 16, pp. 1156-1168, 2021, doi: 10.1109/TIFS.2020.3037006.

[3] J. L. Bentley, “Multidimensional Binary Search Trees Used for Associative Searching,” Stanford University, Stanford, CA, USA, Tech. Rep. STAN-CS-74-354, 1975.

[4] H. Santoso, “Combining Dynamic K-Means and Binary Search Centroid to Optimize Clustering Results on Home Industry Datasets,” 2018 International Conference on Information Management and Technology (ICIMTech), Jakarta, Indonesia, 2018, pp. 1-6, doi: 10.1109/ICIMTech.2018.8527099.
